IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper targets an important problem (knowledge-guided sequence tagging) and proposes a practically useful architecture (K-SAN) that could influence applied SLU work, particularly by showing compatibility with different knowledge schemas (dependency trees, AMR). However, from the provided summary it appears largely empirical on ATIS without the formal/theoretical analysis, per-component ablation evidence, or complexity proofs my methodology requires; claims of generality and efficiency are not accompanied by rigorous guarantees. As a result it opens plausible new directions for integrating explicit knowledge into attention models but is unlikely to be immediately transformative without stronger theoretical grounding and broader validation. | B(3): This paper tackles an important practical problem—injecting external structural knowledge into sequence tagging for SLU—using a modular, end-to-end attention mechanism that cleanly accommodates different knowledge forms (dependency paths, AMR) and claims efficiency via parallel substructure encoding. From an applied-NLP perspective the approach is compelling and likely to influence practitioners seeking robust, knowledge-aware taggers, but its impact is tempered by evaluation only on ATIS and no evidence of broad gains across more diverse, large-scale benchmarks. The idea opens reasonable follow-on work (other knowledge types, scaling studies, integration into production pipelines), so I view it as a useful, moderate-impact contribution rather than transformative.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper targets an important problem (knowledge-guided sequence tagging) and proposes a practically useful architecture (K-SAN) that could influence applied SLU work, particularly by showing compatibility with different knowledge schemas (dependency trees, AMR). However, from the provided summary it appears largely empirical on ATIS without the formal/theoretical analysis, per-component ablation evidence, or complexity proofs my methodology requires; claims of generality and efficiency are not accompanied by rigorous guarantees. As a result it opens plausible new directions for integrating explicit knowledge into attention models but is unlikely to be immediately transformative without stronger theoretical grounding and broader validation.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important practical problem—injecting external structural knowledge into sequence tagging for SLU—using a modular, end-to-end attention mechanism that cleanly accommodates different knowledge forms (dependency paths, AMR) and claims efficiency via parallel substructure encoding. From an applied-NLP perspective the approach is compelling and likely to influence practitioners seeking robust, knowledge-aware taggers, but its impact is tempered by evaluation only on ATIS and no evidence of broad gains across more diverse, large-scale benchmarks. The idea opens reasonable follow-on work (other knowledge types, scaling studies, integration into production pipelines), so I view it as a useful, moderate-impact contribution rather than transformative.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares to a reasonable set of established baselines (CRF, RNN tagger, CNN encoder-tagger, DCNN, Tree-RNN) on the standard ATIS splits using the same F1 metric, which indicates largely fair experimental comparisons. However, the provided summary does not indicate explicit acknowledgment when prior methods perform comparably nor detailed per-component ablation isolating the contribution of the knowledge-guided attention (a critical omission for attribution in my theory-first view). Given the otherwise appropriate and relevant baselines, I rate comparisons as good but with minor omissions in analysis and ablations. | B(4): The paper compares K-SAN against appropriate and competitive baselines (CRF, RNN, CNN, DCNN, Tree-RNN) on the same ATIS datasets using F1, so comparisons appear fair. The manuscript discusses prior feature-enrichment limitations and contrasts K-SAN’s structural attention and efficiency claims with tree-based models, and related work coverage is reported as thorough. However, the summary does not explicitly state whether prior methods sometimes match or outperform K-SAN or present detailed ablation contrasts with all strong neural baselines, so I mark one point down for minor omissions in comparative analysis.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares to a reasonable set of established baselines (CRF, RNN tagger, CNN encoder-tagger, DCNN, Tree-RNN) on the standard ATIS splits using the same F1 metric, which indicates largely fair experimental comparisons. However, the provided summary does not indicate explicit acknowledgment when prior methods perform comparably nor detailed per-component ablation isolating the contribution of the knowledge-guided attention (a critical omission for attribution in my theory-first view). Given the otherwise appropriate and relevant baselines, I rate comparisons as good but with minor omissions in analysis and ablations.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares K-SAN against appropriate and competitive baselines (CRF, RNN, CNN, DCNN, Tree-RNN) on the same ATIS datasets using F1, so comparisons appear fair. The manuscript discusses prior feature-enrichment limitations and contrasts K-SAN’s structural attention and efficiency claims with tree-based models, and related work coverage is reported as thorough. However, the summary does not explicitly state whether prior methods sometimes match or outperform K-SAN or present detailed ablation contrasts with all strong neural baselines, so I mark one point down for minor omissions in comparative analysis.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper states clear claims and provides experimental comparisons on ATIS against appropriate baselines, and reports hyperparameters, which supports empirical effectiveness. However, from the provided summary there is no formal/theoretical analysis or complexity proof justifying the efficiency and generalization claims, and no mention of rigorous per-component ablation experiments isolating the contribution of the knowledge-guided attention vs other modules. These gaps constitute important methodological concerns under my theory-first criteria, so while the empirical results are promising the technical evidence is incomplete. | B(3): The paper states clear claims and supports them with experimental results on ATIS comparing to competitive baselines (CRF, RNN, CNN, DCNN, Tree-RNN), which lends empirical support to the main proposal. However, mathematical content appears minimal so there are no derivations to verify, and the experimental methodology has gaps: only a single dataset (ATIS) is reported and no statistical significance tests or runtime/efficiency measurements are described to substantiate the parallelizability claim. There are also some unsupported generalization assertions (e.g., applicability to multiple knowledge formats) that aren’t shown convincingly in the experiments, so I judge the technical evidence as mixed rather than fully convincing.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear claims and provides experimental comparisons on ATIS against appropriate baselines, and reports hyperparameters, which supports empirical effectiveness. However, from the provided summary there is no formal/theoretical analysis or complexity proof justifying the efficiency and generalization claims, and no mention of rigorous per-component ablation experiments isolating the contribution of the knowledge-guided attention vs other modules. These gaps constitute important methodological concerns under my theory-first criteria, so while the empirical results are promising the technical evidence is incomplete.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear claims and supports them with experimental results on ATIS comparing to competitive baselines (CRF, RNN, CNN, DCNN, Tree-RNN), which lends empirical support to the main proposal. However, mathematical content appears minimal so there are no derivations to verify, and the experimental methodology has gaps: only a single dataset (ATIS) is reported and no statistical significance tests or runtime/efficiency measurements are described to substantiate the parallelizability claim. There are also some unsupported generalization assertions (e.g., applicability to multiple knowledge formats) that aren’t shown convincingly in the experiments, so I judge the technical evidence as mixed rather than fully convincing.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is a good fit for top NLP/ML venues, presenting a practical, modular knowledge-guided attention model evaluated against strong baselines on a standard task. The main caveat is limited empirical validation (single-domain ATIS) and the lack of formal analysis or detailed ablations to fully isolate contributions, which prevents a higher score but does not undermine overall appropriateness for the venue.

reviewer_a.justification: The topic (knowledge-guided attention for sequence tagging) and the ATIS slot-filling experiments are squarely within top NLP/ML conference scope and target the right audience, and the paper uses standard baselines and F1 evaluation. However, from the summary the work appears primarily empirical on a single domain (ATIS) with no mention of formal/theoretical motivation, guarantees, or complexity analysis and no explicit per-component ablation studies to isolate the contribution of the proposed structural-attention components. The claim of generality to different knowledge schemas (dependency trees, AMR) and of efficiency/parallelizability is asserted but not supported by formal definitions or complexity proofs in the summary, so the fit is borderline rather than a clear strong match.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper fits well within top-tier NLP/ML venues: it targets spoken language understanding and proposes a practical, modular knowledge-guided attention model (K-SAN) that integrates external structure (dependency/AMR) into sequence tagging. The methodology and evaluation follow community standards (GRU-based tagger, comparisons to strong baselines like RNN/Tree-RNN/CNN, F1 on ATIS) and the focus on scalability and modularity aligns with applied research priorities. My only mild reservation is reliance on a single domain (ATIS) which may limit claims of broad empirical validation, but the contribution and audience match the venue.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper composes existing primitives (attention, RNNs, external parses) rather than introducing a wholly new paradigm, but they also concur it brings a meaningful methodological contribution by framing external knowledge as a ‘teacher’ guiding attention over substructures and offering a broadly applicable, parallelizable design. Given this balance—non-trivial and practically useful novelty but limited theoretical depth—I rate originality as above average (4/5).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a concrete new architecture (K-SAN) that applies knowledge-guided attention over sentence substructures (e.g., dependency paths, AMR) to improve slot tagging—this is a non-trivial, application-specific combination of external knowledge encoding and attention. However, the core ingredients (attention, RNN/GRU taggers, substructure encoders) are existing techniques and the work reads as an engineering-oriented integration rather than a fundamentally new modeling paradigm. I downgrade from higher novelty because claims of generality across knowledge schemas and efficiency are empirical and lack formal definitions, theoretical analysis, or complexity proofs that would substantiate a genuinely new methodological contribution to the field.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable novelty by framing external linguistic knowledge as a ‘teacher’ that guides an attention mechanism over sentence substructures (e.g., dependency paths, AMR) to improve sequence tagging, which is distinct from prior feature-enrichment or pure tree-RNN approaches. It clearly differentiates itself from earlier work (CRF/RNN/Tree-RNN/CNN baselines) and claims a broadly applicable design across different knowledge representations, which is a non-trivial methodological extension beyond existing methods. From an applied perspective, the emphasis on parallelizable substructure encoding and end-to-end learning would be informative to practitioners, even if the core components (attention + RNN + external parse) build on established primitives.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and readable: the summary indicates a clear intro-method-experiments-conclusion flow, main contributions are stated early, and writing quality is reported as good. Notation and methodological descriptions (encoding substructures, attention, RNN tagger with GRU) appear consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables (datasets, baselines, F1 results) seem informative. Minor presentation shortcomings: claims about generality and efficiency would benefit from clearer, formal definitions or precise statements to avoid ambiguity. From a presentation standpoint it is strong with only small clarifications needed. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and presents a clear logical flow from motivation to method to experiments and conclusion, with the main contributions stated early (K-SAN, knowledge-as-teacher idea, and efficiency claims). Notation and methodological descriptions are consistent enough to understand the encoding, attention, and RNN tagger pipeline, and hyperparameters and related work are reported thoroughly. Figures/tables/examples appear to support the empirical claims (ATIS experiments and baselines) though the summary does not detail them, so I reserve a perfect score for minor presentation details that could be more explicitly highlighted for readers reproducing the work.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and readable: the summary indicates a clear intro-method-experiments-conclusion flow, main contributions are stated early, and writing quality is reported as good. Notation and methodological descriptions (encoding substructures, attention, RNN tagger with GRU) appear consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables (datasets, baselines, F1 results) seem informative. Minor presentation shortcomings: claims about generality and efficiency would benefit from clearer, formal definitions or precise statements to avoid ambiguity. From a presentation standpoint it is strong with only small clarifications needed.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and presents a clear logical flow from motivation to method to experiments and conclusion, with the main contributions stated early (K-SAN, knowledge-as-teacher idea, and efficiency claims). Notation and methodological descriptions are consistent enough to understand the encoding, attention, and RNN tagger pipeline, and hyperparameters and related work are reported thoroughly. Figures/tables/examples appear to support the empirical claims (ATIS experiments and baselines) though the summary does not detail them, so I reserve a perfect score for minor presentation details that could be more explicitly highlighted for readers reproducing the work.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper demonstrates reasonable empirical gains on ATIS with reported hyperparameters, but that evaluation is narrowly focused on a single domain and lacks comprehensive ablation, sensitivity, and runtime analyses as well as discussion of failure modes and code release. Given the supported core claims but limited generalization evidence and missing deeper analyses, a middle score reflects the paper’s meaningful contribution tempered by insufficient evaluation depth and reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: From a theory-first perspective the experimental validation is shallow: evaluation is limited to ATIS variants (narrow domain) which weakens the paper's generalization claims about different knowledge schemas. The summary does not report rigorous ablation studies isolating each architectural component (attention teacher, substructure encoders, hybrid RNN tagger) nor formal complexity analysis to substantiate efficiency/parallelism claims. Related work coverage is said to be thorough, but failure modes and negative results for K-SAN itself are not discussed and code isn’t reported (only hyperparameters). Reproducibility is partially supported by hyperparameter reporting but key analyses required by my reviewer persona are missing, so overall depth is insufficient.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents reasonable experimental comparisons against multiple strong baselines on ATIS and reports hyperparameters, which supports core claims about K-SAN’s effectiveness; however evaluation is limited to a single domain/dataset so generalization claims (different knowledge types, efficiency at scale) are under-tested. Ablation/sensitivity analyses are not clearly stated in the summary (so key questions about the contribution of the attention/knowledge components and runtime trade-offs remain insufficiently explored). Related work coverage is said to be thorough, but discussion of the method’s own failure cases and broader limitations appears minimal, and code release is not mentioned, which reduces reproducibility despite reported hyperparameters.

main_claims: Introduces K-SAN, a knowledge-guided structural attention network that uses external/prior knowledge to guide attention over sentence substructures for sequence tagging.
Proposes an end-to-end neural approach where knowledge acts as a 'teacher' to automatically learn important substructures via an attention mechanism.
Claims the model generalizes to different kinds of knowledge representations (e.g., dependency trees, AMR) without requiring a specific knowledge schema.
Argues the model is efficient and parallelizable because substructures are modeled separately, mitigating linear growth of modeling time with sentence length.
_summary.methodology: K-SAN encodes knowledge-guided substructures (e.g., dependency paths or AMR subgraphs) into vector representations, applies an attention mechanism to produce a knowledge-guided sentence representation, and feeds this representation into an RNN (with GRU cells) tagger to predict per-token semantic slot labels.
datasets: ATIS (benchmark ATIS corpus, ATIS-2 and ATIS-3 subsets)
limitations: Prior feature-enrichment based approaches suffer from poor generalization and error propagation (stated as limitations of previous work)
_summary.topic_area: NLP - spoken language understanding / slot filling (sequence labeling)

reviewer_a.name: Theory-First Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Professor of machine learning and formal methods with extensive experience in theoretical analysis of neural architectures and attention mechanisms.
traits: Penalizes empirical papers that lack formal/theoretical analysis or guarantees (subtracts points if no theoretical motivation or complexity proof).
Requires rigorous ablation studies isolating each architectural component (expects per-component removal results).
Penalizes papers that make strong claims about generality without formal definitions or proofs (reduces score when 'generalizes to different knowledge schemas' is only empirically shown).
Demands clear computational complexity analysis and evidence for efficiency claims (lowers score if only qualitative or limited benchmarking is provided).
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on theoretical soundness and clear causal attribution of improvements; downgrades papers that are primarily engineering/empirical without formal justification.

reviewer_b.name: Applied NLP Systems Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher in spoken language understanding and industrial NLP, with a track record of deploying sequence-labeling models in production conversational agents.
traits: Rewards practical, end-to-end methods that improve real-world task metrics and robustness (gives bonus for consistent empirical gains across datasets).
Values modular approaches that leverage external knowledge and are easy to integrate into existing pipelines (raises score if method cleanly accommodates multiple knowledge formats).
Prioritizes empirical evidence of efficiency and scalability in realistic settings (rewards papers that demonstrate parallelizability or reduced runtime/memory in experiments).
Gives credit for strong experimental design including comparisons to competitive neural baselines and reasonable ablation studies (but tolerant if not every theoretical aspect is covered).
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Pragmatic evaluator who privileges empirical effectiveness, applicability, and engineering clarity; willing to accept limited theoretical analysis if practical benefits are clear.
